back: A top-down approach where a higher-level entity is divided into lower-level entities based on specific attributes. For example, a PERSON entity can be specialized into TEACHER and STUDENT.
A bottom-up approach where multiple lower-level entities are combined to form a higher-level entity. For example, CAR and TRUCK can be generalized into VEHICLE.
ISA stands for 'is-a' relationship, representing the hierarchical relationship between a superclass and its subclasses in specialization/generalization.
A property where subclasses automatically inherit all attributes and relationships of their superclass. For example, if STUDENT inherits from PERSON, it gets name and address automatically.

front: What is a superclass (or super entity)?
back: The higher-level entity in a specialization/generalization hierarchy that contains common attributes shared by all its subclasses.

front: What is a subclass (or sub entity)?
back: The lower-level entity that inherits attributes from the superclass and may have its own specific attributes.

front: What is the difference between disjoint and overlapping constraints?
back: Disjoint (or exclusive) means a member of superclass can belong to only one subclass. Overlapping means a member can belong to multiple subclasses simultaneously.
Every entity in the superclass must participate in the ISA relationship as a member of some subclass. Shown by double line in ER diagram.
Some entities in the superclass may not belong to any subclass. Shown by single line in ER diagram.

front: What is a category in ER modeling?
back: A union type where a subclass is defined as a union of two or more superclasses. Represented as U in circle notation.

front: What is the difference between specialization and generalization?
back: Specialization starts from superclass and creates subclasses (top-down). Generalization combines subclasses into a superclass (bottom-up). They are reverse processes.
A subclass defined by a predicate condition on the superclass attribute. For example, EMPLOYEE with salary > 50000 becomes MANAGER subclass.

front: How is specialization/generalization represented in ER diagrams?
back: Using a triangle with 'ISA' label connecting the superclass to its subclasses. Attributes of subclasses are listed below each subclass.
The arrangement of entities in layers from general (root) to specific (leaves), showing inheritance relationships in a tree-like structure.

front: Why use specialization/generalization in database design?
back: To reduce redundancy by extracting common attributes, represent real-world relationships accurately, and maintain data integrity through inheritance.
